even make those worse off. This means that they 
are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”.  Any effort to generate 
lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is 
evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is 
not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory 
or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. 
 
This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must 
be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat 
as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the 
broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more 
specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Table 2: The Four Problem Types- Expanded Distinctions 
 
Utility dominates? 
Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and 
states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind 
“utility enhancing” outcome?

er attention to “excludability”, this school does the same to understand how, not whether, a 
particular environmental problem can be solved. For example, my careful attention to this question with 
Ishani Mukherjee, Lahiru Wijedasa and Altaf Virani, to Peatland, found that a key features was that its 
key features meant that it was impossible to maintain with irrigation – which would ultimately produce 
resource collapse (Cashore et al. 2024). Likewise this classification is consistent with those seeking to 
avoid erosion of hillside streams which the science shows that if policies must conform to the key feature 
that any number of trees must not be removed (Sabatier and Jenkins-Smith 1993). Hence this school – 
whether looking at a particular species or the 1.5 degree imperative spends careful time on the nature of 
the problem, and works inductively, or teleologically, to uncover those key features that would need to be 
ascertained to effectively manage the problem. 
 
 
Utility dominates? No. In fact, this school has documented overwhelming evidence corroborating 
Cashore’s Chapter One, that almost all Type 4 problems almost always result from, or are exacerbated by, 
highly effective Types 3, 2 and 1 policy solutions (Sinden, Kysar, and Driesen 2009; Zeckhauser and 
Schaefer 1968; Ackerman and Heinzerling 2004).  
 
Solution orientation: confronts “commensurability” biases (Tribe 1972) inherent in the approach of the 
Commons and Optimization schools to instead, advancing a “sequentialist” approach that ranks “on the 
ground” problems in order of importance to policy makers and society, rather than turning to cost-benefit 
or multi-goal policy analysis to make these choices for them (Cashore and Bernstein 2022).   
a classic example concerns anti-slavery as a policy objective that trumps utility calculations or 
compromise solutions (Cashore and Bernstein 2022). Hence, the prioritization school’s approach narrows 
solutions capable of achieving, rather than c

a particular “on the ground” problem. That 
is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the 
problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed?  
 
As of September 12, 2025 2:27 pm 
 
- 5 -
This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to 
understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular 
species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the 
Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to 
implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. 
Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem 
at hand ought to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for 
other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem.  
 
Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a 
theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems 
from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully 
assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to 
better manage or solve.  
 
This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is 
because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively 
manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they 
are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”.  Any effort to generate 
lessons beyond a class of problems that is offe

are related to an improvement in participants’ behavior of at least 20 percent since they joined the voluntary
urban climate program (O2). The solution coverage and solution consistency are again high (both 1.00).
Table 7 indicates that seven conﬁgurations in three clusters (VII.i–VII.iv, VIII.i, VIII.ii, and IX) are related to a
failure to attract at least 15 percent of the pool of prospective participants (~O1). The solution coverage and solu-
tion consistency are both high (1.00). Table 7 further indicates that two conﬁgurations (X and XI) are related to a
lack of improvement in participants’ behavior of at least 20 percent since they joined the voluntary urban climate
program (~O2). The solution coverage and solution consistency are high (1.00). Tables 6 and 7 further indicate
core causal conditions that must be present or absent in causing the outcome (indicated by large circles in the
tables), and contributing causal conditions (indicated by small circles in the tables). The core causal conditions in
Tables 6 and 7 present what in QCA is termed the “intermediary solution,” while the core causal conditions com-
bined with the contributing causal conditions present the “complex solution.” The complex solution is exclusively
based on the empirical information at hand. The complex solution can, however, be further simpliﬁed by using
counterfactuals. For this study the counterfactuals used were based on the assumptions identiﬁed in the literature
discussed earlier in the article, resulting in the intermediary solution (Fiss 2011). It should be noted that, while the
interme

factuals used were based on the assumptions identiﬁed in the literature
discussed earlier in the article, resulting in the intermediary solution (Fiss 2011). It should be noted that, while the
intermediary solution may look “simpler” than the complex solution, it provides less categorical delineation.
5. Discussion: Further exploration of the diffusion network perspective
The raw data (Table 2) provide empirical evidence for a recurring assumption in the literature on voluntary pro-
grams. Voluntary programs that indicate good performance in terms of attracting participants by no means
always also indicate good performance in changing the participants’ behavior, and vice versa. This indicates that
various metrics are required to assess the overall performance of a voluntary program – that is, the extent to
which it helps to address the societal problem it targets (Borck & Coglianese 2009; Potoski & Prakash 2009; van
der Heijden 2012). The iterative steps of data–theory matching provide evidence for other core assumptions in
the literature on voluntary program performance. That is, various conditions interact in how they affect program
performance (conjunctural causation), and different theoretical models can explain program performance (equi-
ﬁnality) (Fowler & Rauch 2006; Thøgersen 2010; Boyd & Ghosh 2013).
